recent_coach_change: Petkovic appointed in 2024 (early 2024), bringing stability after federation turmoil; no change since.
qualification_path: Won their CAF (African) qualifying group with eight wins and one draw, scoring 24 goals; sealed their place with a 3-0 win over Somalia. First World Cup since 2014, their fifth appearance overall.
squad_notes: Notable inclusion of Luca Zidane (son of Zinedine) in goal after his France-to-Algeria switch in September 2025, plus emerging midfielder Ibrahim Maza (Bayer Leverkusen). Squad leans on a strong European-based diaspora.
warmup_assessment: Very encouraging. Beat the Netherlands 1-0 in Rotterdam (Anis Hadj-Moussa 85th minute) defending resolutely with Luca Zidane outstanding; close out warm-ups vs Bolivia on 10 June in Kansas City.
shape_verdict: Algeria arrive in confident, settled shape after a dominant qualifying campaign and an eye-catching win over the Netherlands; Petkovic has restored stability and the attack looks sharp.
warmup_results: 2026-06-03 @ Netherlands 1-0 W | 2026-03-31 vN Uruguay 0-0 D | 2026-03-27 vN Guatemala 7-0 W

recent_coach_change: No change; Scaloni continues since 2018 and won the 2022 World Cup and 2024 Copa America.
qualification_path: Qualified via CONMEBOL South American qualifying, finishing top of the table well ahead of the field as the first nation to secure a 2026 berth.
key_injuries: Lionel Messi (doubt); Emiliano Martinez (doubt); Cristian Romero (doubt); Nahuel Molina (doubt); Gonzalo Montiel (doubt); Juan Foyth (out)
squad_notes: Squad built on continuity: 17 of the 26 are 2022 Qatar champions. Paulo Dybala omitted (no call-up since September 2024). Giovani Lo Celso returns after missing 2022 through injury. Scaloni delayed the final 26 to the last minute to assess fitness of several core players.
warmup_assessment: Solid. Beat Honduras 2-0 on 6 June (Lautaro Martinez penalty, Giuliano Simeone) with Messi rested; a second friendly vs Iceland follows on 9 June. Scaloni rotated and avoided risking fitness-doubt players.
shape_verdict: Defending champions look in good collective shape with depth and continuity, though several key starters including Messi, Emi Martinez and Romero are managing knocks heading into the opener.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Honduras 2-0 W | 2026-03-31 vs Zambia 5-0 W | 2026-03-27 vs Mauritania 2-1 W

recent_coach_change: Popovic took over in September 2024, replacing Graham Arnold; led Australia on an eight-game unbeaten run to direct qualification. No change since.
base_formation: Balanced structure built around a veteran spine; back four with experienced GK Mathew Ryan
qualification_path: Qualified directly via AFC third round (Group C runners-up), reaching the World Cup without a playoff for the first time since 2014.
key_injuries: Riley McGree (out); Hayden Matthews (out); Nick D'Agostino (out)
squad_notes: Popovic named his 26-man squad on June 1, 2026 after an extended camp in Sarasota, Florida (running since April), rotating eight players in and eight out. Cristian Volpato (Sassuolo) was a notable inclusion after switching allegiance from Italy; Tete Yengi was also a first-time call-up. Harry Souttar and Matthew Leckie returned from long absences, as did Jackson Irvine, Mohamed Toure and Cameron Devlin. Veteran Martin Boyle was omitted. 17 players set for World Cup debuts.
warmup_assessment: Underwhelming final tune-up. Australia lost 1-0 to Mexico in their last friendly on June 2, 2026. Leckie's appearance in that match was his first since June 2024 following hip surgery in December 2025.
shape_verdict: An experienced, well-drilled but goal-light Socceroos side leaning on a veteran spine and youthful wide threats. Losing creator Riley McGree to injury on the eve of the tournament dents their attacking depth.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Switzerland 1-1 D | 2026-05-30 vN Mexico 0-1 L

recent_coach_change: No change; Rangnick has led Austria since 2022 and reportedly turned down a Bayern Munich approach to stay through World Cup qualification.
qualification_path: Topped UEFA Group H with 19 points from eight matches, two clear of Bosnia and Herzegovina; included a 10-0 win over San Marino. First World Cup appearance in 28 years (since 1998).
squad_notes: Eye-catching naturalised inclusions Paul Wanner (Vienna-born, ex-Germany youth) and Carney Chukwuemeka (Vienna-born, ex-England youth), both personally recruited by Rangnick. Alaba returns as captain for his first World Cup at 33.
warmup_assessment: Functional. Beat Tunisia 1-0 in Vienna on 1 June; arrived on the back of winning eight of their last ten including wins over Ghana and South Korea, though the attack relies on the ageing Arnautovic.
shape_verdict: Austria look organised and in good form under Rangnick's intense pressing system, but questions remain over attacking depth and the fitness durability of captain Alaba.
warmup_results: 2026-06-01 vs Tunisia 1-0 W | 2026-03-31 vs South Korea 1-0 W | 2026-03-27 vs Ghana 5-1 W

recent_coach_change: Yes — Rudi Garcia appointed in January 2025, replacing Domenico Tedesco, on a contract running through the 2026 World Cup.
qualification_path: Qualified by topping UEFA qualifying Group J unbeaten (W5 D3 L0, 29 goals for, 7 against), sealing it with a 7-0 win over Liechtenstein; group also contained Wales, North Macedonia and Kazakhstan.
squad_notes: Garcia kept faith with the golden-generation veterans (De Bruyne, Lukaku, Courtois) while blending younger talent. Youri Tielemans was handed the captain's armband. The biggest talking point was the omission of qualifying top scorer Malick Fofana.
warmup_assessment: Mixed warm-ups: a 0-0 draw away to North Macedonia in Skopje on June 6, 2026, then a 4-3 win over Wales in Brussels on June 9. Attacking output was strong but the defense looked leaky.
shape_verdict: A deep, experienced squad and a comfortable qualifying campaign make Belgium clear Group G favorites, but an aging core and shaky defensive showings in warm-ups leave questions over their ceiling.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vs Tunisia 5-0 W | 2026-06-02 @ Croatia 2-0 W | 2026-03-31 vN Mexico 1-1 D | 2026-03-28 @ United States 5-2 W

recent_coach_change: Barbarez took the job on April 19, 2024 on a four-year deal and guided Bosnia to qualification; no change since.
qualification_path: Qualified via the UEFA play-offs: beat Wales on penalties in the semi-final, then defeated four-time champions Italy on penalties in the play-off final.
squad_notes: Barbarez named his final 26 on May 11, 2026, headlined by 40-year-old Edin Dzeko. Goalkeeper Osman Hadzikic withdrew injured and was replaced by 18-year-old Mladen Jurkas on June 1. Both Demirovic and Tabakovic made the cut up front.
warmup_assessment: Underwhelming: Bosnia drew 1-1 with Panama in St. Louis on June 6, 2026 (Nikola Katic scoring, Jiovany Ramos equalizing), and were without injured striker Haris Tabakovic.
shape_verdict: A spirited side riding the high of a dramatic playoff run past Wales and Italy, but reliant on a 40-year-old Dzeko and looking less than fluent after a 1-1 draw with Panama.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Panama 1-1 D | 2026-05-29 vs North Macedonia 0-0 D

recent_coach_change: Yes. Dorival Junior was sacked after a poor CONMEBOL qualifying campaign and Carlo Ancelotti took over with an official start date of 26 May 2025, becoming Brazil's first permanent foreign manager.
base_formation: 4-2-3-1 / flexible attacking setup, with Ancelotti having trialled Vinicius Jr as a central striker
qualification_path: Qualified via CONMEBOL South American qualifying; finished fifth in the table, a campaign described as underwhelming that cost Dorival Junior his job.
squad_notes: Headline story is the surprise inclusion of 34-year-old Neymar (Santos) despite limited recent minutes and a fresh calf injury; he effectively took Estevao's spot. Joao Pedro (Chelsea) was cut to make room. Brazil go in without injured trio Estevao, Rodrygo and Eder Militao.
warmup_assessment: Strong. Brazil thrashed Panama 6-2 (Vinicius, Casemiro and Paqueta among the scorers) and beat Egypt 2-1 (Bruno Guimaraes scoring), showing fluid attacking play under Ancelotti despite Neymar's absence from both.
shape_verdict: Brazil look sharp and confident going in, with a settled Ancelotti attacking identity and two winning warm-ups, but the injury losses of Rodrygo, Militao and Estevao plus uncertainty over Neymar's fitness are real caveats.

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Marsch (appointed 2024) signed a contract extension ahead of the tournament and leads Canada into his first World Cup as host-nation manager.
qualification_path: Automatic qualification as co-host nation (Canada, Mexico, USA host 2026).
key_injuries: Alphonso Davies (doubt); Marcelo Flores (out); Moise Bombito (returned); Promise David (returned)
squad_notes: Marsch revealed his final 26 (3 GK, 9 DF, 10 MF, 4 FW) on May 29, 2026 via a CN Tower TV special after a 32-man preliminary squad on May 25. He called it the best squad Canada has assembled. Injuries shaped the selection, most notably Marcelo Flores withdrawing with an ACL tear; Maxime Crepeau and Dayne St. Clair competed for the No. 1 spot.
warmup_assessment: Positive momentum: Canada beat Uzbekistan 2-0 in Edmonton on June 1, 2026 (Jonathan Osorio and Jayden Nelson scoring), with a final tune-up against the Republic of Ireland before the June 12 opener.
shape_verdict: Canada look upbeat and well-organized under Marsch as hosts, but the squad is four years older than 2022 and the fitness of captain Alphonso Davies is the key unknown.
warmup_results: 2026-06-05 vs Republic of Ireland 1-1 D | 2026-06-01 vs Uzbekistan 2-0 W | 2026-03-31 vs Tunisia 0-0 D | 2026-03-28 vs Iceland 2-2 D

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Bubista has been in charge since 2020 and led the country to its first-ever World Cup.
qualification_path: Qualified for their first-ever World Cup by winning CAF qualifying Group D in October 2025.
squad_notes: First World Cup appearance for the nation of roughly 600,000 (third-smallest World Cup country). Squad is heavily diaspora-based (players born in France, Portugal, Netherlands, Ireland). Bubista kept the core that secured qualification; Logan Costa's inclusion despite minimal match fitness is the headline gamble. Carlos dos Santos is the only uncapped player.
warmup_assessment: Excellent and confidence-building: Cape Verde won both June warm-ups 3-0, beating Serbia in Lisbon on May 31, 2026 and Bermuda in Connecticut on June 6, 2026.
shape_verdict: The debutants arrive in buoyant form after two convincing 3-0 warm-up wins, though they are the lowest-ranked side in Group H and lean on an injury-doubtful Logan Costa at the back.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Bermuda 0-3 L | 2026-05-31 vN Serbia 3-0 W

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Argentine Nestor Lorenzo has led Colombia since June 2022 (succeeding Reinaldo Rueda) and oversaw qualification.
qualification_path: Finished a strong third (some sources second) in CONMEBOL qualifying with around 28 points from 18 matches, securing automatic qualification behind Argentina. Second-best defensive record in CONMEBOL.
squad_notes: Lorenzo named his 26-man squad on 25 May 2026. James Rodriguez retained as captain despite limited minutes, with the staff running a special physical program for him. Notable omissions include Wilmar Barrios (Zenit) and Rafael Santos Borre. Real Betis forward 'Cucho' Hernandez among the in-form inclusions.
warmup_assessment: Strong recent form: thrashed Mexico 4-0 and beat Australia 3-0 in late-2025 friendlies, showing attacking sharpness and defensive solidity ahead of the tournament.
shape_verdict: Colombia look in good shape as group favourites alongside Portugal, blending Luis Diaz's quality with a solid defence; the main question mark is the fitness and form of veteran captain James Rodriguez.
warmup_results: 2026-06-01 vs Costa Rica 3-1 W | 2026-03-29 vN France 1-3 L | 2026-03-26 vN Croatia 1-2 L

recent_coach_change: None recent. Dalic has been in charge since 7 October 2017, when he replaced the sacked Ante Cacic; he remains Croatia's longest-serving and most successful coach.
base_formation: 4-3-3 (often shifting to a 4-2-3-1), built around a veteran central midfield
qualification_path: Won UEFA Group L with a record of P8 W7 D1 L0, 26 goals for and 4 against; group runners-up were the Czech Republic. Sealed direct qualification in November 2025.
squad_notes: Dalic blended youth and experience. Veterans Luka Modric (captain), Ivan Perisic, Mario Pasalic and Andrej Kramaric were all retained. Gvardiol returned from a broken shin and Kovacic recovered from injury in time. Seven standby players were also named alongside the 26-man squad announced on 18 May 2026.
warmup_assessment: Mixed. Croatia lost their first warm-up 0-2 at home to Belgium on 2 June 2026, with Modric starting. A final home tune-up against Slovenia was scheduled for 7 June in Varazdin (result not yet available at snapshot).
shape_verdict: An experienced, well-organised side that qualified comfortably, but the ageing core and a flat 0-2 loss to Belgium in their opening warm-up raise questions about whether they can match younger, faster opponents.
warmup_results: 2026-06-02 vs Belgium 0-2 L | 2026-03-31 vN Brazil 1-3 L | 2026-03-26 vN Colombia 2-1 W

recent_coach_change: Major recent saga: Advocaat guided Curacao through qualification, then stepped down early 2026 over his daughter's health. Fred Rutten took over but parted ways after two heavy March friendly defeats and player-council pressure; Advocaat was reappointed in May 2026 to lead the team at the finals, becoming the oldest head coach (78) ever at a World Cup.
qualification_path: First-ever World Cup qualification via CONCACAF: won all four matches of their initial group, then topped the final group (above Jamaica and Trinidad & Tobago) to claim a direct berth
squad_notes: Curacao will be the smallest country (population ~150,000) ever to play at a World Cup and one of the most inexperienced squads, leaning heavily on Dutch-developed players of Curacaoan descent eligible via the FIFA rule on youth-level representation.
warmup_assessment: Poor and mixed: heavy March defeats (5-1 to Australia, 2-0 to China) helped trigger the coaching change, then a 4-1 loss to Scotland on 30 May; only a 4-0 win over minnows Aruba (6 June) offered late respite.
shape_verdict: A historic, feel-good debutant but in shaky form after chastening warm-up defeats and a destabilising coaching merry-go-round; Advocaat's return brings calm but expectations are very low in a brutal group.

recent_coach_change: Yes — Miroslav Koubek replaced Ivan Hasek in December 2025. At 74, Koubek is the oldest manager at the 2026 World Cup; he is a former goalkeeper who won the Czech league with Viktoria Plzen.
qualification_path: Qualified via the European playoffs, beating Republic of Ireland (2-2, won 4-3 on penalties on 26 March) and then Denmark (2-2, won 3-1 on penalties on 31 March), both in Prague and both on penalty shootouts.
squad_notes: Koubek named the final 26 on 31 May 2026, with Wolves defender Ladislav Krejci as captain. Spine of the side: Matej Kovar in goal, Tomas Holes in defence, Tomas Soucek in midfield and Patrik Schick up front, plus Adam Hlozek, Pavel Sulc and Lukas Provod. Hlozek's return from a long injury layoff was a notable inclusion.
warmup_assessment: Solid finish to warm-ups: Czechia beat Guatemala 3-1 on 4 June 2026 in Harrison, New Jersey (Schick, Tomas Chory and Denis Visinsky scoring), after an earlier match against Kosovo in which Jan Kuchta picked up an ankle knock.
shape_verdict: Czechia return to the World Cup after a 16-year absence carrying late momentum from a 3-1 win over Guatemala, with Schick fit and firing and Hlozek back from injury, though they only scraped through via two playoff penalty shootouts under new 74-year-old coach Miroslav Koubek.
warmup_results: 2026-06-04 vN Guatemala 5-1 W | 2026-05-31 vs Kosovo 2-1 W

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Desabre has led the Leopards since August 2022 (succeeding Hector Cuper) and guided their return to the World Cup.
qualification_path: Finished second in CAF qualifying Group B behind Senegal (22 points), then won the CAF play-off mini-tournament in Morocco (beat Cameroon and Nigeria on penalties) before defeating Jamaica 1-0 in the intercontinental play-off final. First World Cup since 1974 (as Zaire).
squad_notes: 26-man squad announced 18 May 2026. Belgium-born Mario Stroeykens and France-born Warren Bondo both miss out after declaring for DR Congo, as does former Manchester United and France youth defender Willy Kambwala. Squad mixes diaspora talent from Europe with the captain Mbemba.
warmup_assessment: Drew 0-0 with Denmark on 3 June 2026 in Liege, a competitive showing with Wissa a constant outlet; a further friendly against Chile was scheduled for 9 June.
shape_verdict: DR Congo arrive as plucky play-off qualifiers on their first World Cup in 52 years, organised under Desabre and led by Mbemba and Wissa, but lacking the elite depth of the group's bigger names.
warmup_results: 2026-06-03 vN Denmark 0-0 D | 2026-03-25 vN Bermuda 2-0 W

recent_coach_change: Beccacece appointed August 2024 (after Copa America); no change since.
qualification_path: Finished 2nd in CONMEBOL qualifying, behind only Argentina, despite starting on -3 points (Byron Castillo/CAS sanction)
squad_notes: Squad blends experienced core (Pacho, Hincapie, Estupinan, Plata, Valencia) with rising talents (Kendry Paez, Joel Ordonez, Yaimar Medina, Jeremy Arevalo). Built on outstanding defensive record in qualifying (only 5 goals conceded across 18 matches).
warmup_assessment: Positive: Ecuador beat Saudi Arabia 2-1 in a 2026 warm-up friendly, maintaining the defensive solidity that defined their qualifying run.
shape_verdict: Ecuador arrive as one of the tournament's surprise packages, exceptionally hard to break down (overcame a -3 point start to finish 2nd in CONMEBOL); the main question is goal output given modest attacking returns.
warmup_results: 2026-05-30 vN Saudi Arabia 2-1 W | 2026-03-31 @ Netherlands 1-1 D | 2026-03-27 vN Morocco 1-1 D

recent_coach_change: No recent change since the start of the WC cycle; Hassan was appointed in February 2024 (replacing Rui Vitoria) and has led the qualifying campaign.
qualification_path: Won CAF Group A unbeaten across all 10 matches (8 wins, 2 draws, 26 points, 20 goals for, 2 against, 7 clean sheets); sealed qualification with a 3-0 win away at Djibouti on October 8, 2025.
squad_notes: Hossam Hassan confirmed his final 26-man squad on May 29, 2026. The biggest talking point was the call-up of 18-year-old Barcelona B prospect Hamza Abdelkarim, with Nantes striker Mostafa Mohamed left out. Salah captains a squad chasing Egypt's first-ever World Cup knockout berth.
warmup_assessment: Lost 2-1 to Brazil in Cleveland, Ohio on June 6, 2026 in their final warm-up; Salah came on at halftime. Egypt competed well and stretched Brazil but couldn't find an equalizer. Earlier 2025-26 form was mixed (Arab Cup group-stage exit; AFCON semi-final loss to Senegal).
shape_verdict: A dominant, defensively miserly qualifying campaign and the world-class Salah-Marmoush axis make Egypt dangerous, but mixed tournament form and a narrow loss to Brazil suggest they remain underdogs against Belgium.

recent_coach_change: Tuchel appointed 16 October 2024 on an 18-month contract, beginning 1 January 2025, succeeding interim boss Lee Carsley after Gareth Southgate stepped down following Euro 2024.
base_formation: Tactically flexible; primarily 4-2-3-1, also using 4-3-3 and back-three variants depending on opponent
qualification_path: Won UEFA Group K with a perfect record: P8 W8 D0 L0, 22 goals for, 0 conceded; sealed qualification on 14 October 2025 with a 5-0 win in Latvia. Only European team to win all its qualifiers.
key_injuries: Tino Livramento (doubt); Djed Spence (doubt); Reece James (returned); John Stones (doubt)
squad_notes: Ruthless selection by Tuchel. Big-name omissions: Phil Foden, Cole Palmer, Harry Maguire, Trent Alexander-Arnold, Morgan Gibbs-White, Luke Shaw, Jarrod Bowen and Lewis Hall. No Liverpool players selected for the first time since 1986. Shock recall for Ivan Toney. Kane equals Billy Wright's record of captaining England at three World Cups; Jordan Henderson set to equal a record fourth finals appearance.
warmup_assessment: Pre-tournament friendlies against New Zealand (Tampa, 6 June) and Costa Rica (Orlando, 10 June). Arsenal contingent (Rice, Saka, Madueke, Eze) joined late after the Champions League final, so England were without them for the first friendly before the group moved to their Kansas City base on 13 June.
shape_verdict: England arrive in excellent shape after a flawless qualifying campaign with the meanest defence in Europe, though a cluster of fullback fitness doubts and Tuchel's ruthless omissions of star names add some uncertainty.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN New Zealand 1-0 W | 2026-03-31 vs Japan 0-1 L | 2026-03-27 vs Uruguay 1-1 D

recent_coach_change: No change. Deschamps has announced the 2026 World Cup will be his final tournament in charge before stepping down.
qualification_path: Qualified through UEFA qualifying as group winners.
squad_notes: Eduardo Camavinga left out after a difficult, injury-affected season with reduced minutes at Real Madrid. Randal Kolo Muani also missed the cut. Squad still regarded as the most richly talented pool in world football.
warmup_assessment: Mixed. France were stunned 2-1 by Ivory Coast in Nantes on 4 June 2026 in their final warm-up; Rayan Cherki opened the scoring but a heavily rotated second-half side conceded twice, raising questions about depth and Deschamps' tactical approach.
shape_verdict: Loaded with talent and among the tournament favourites, but the late friendly defeat to Ivory Coast exposed vulnerability when rotating and questions persist over whether Deschamps maximises the squad.
warmup_results: 2026-06-04 vs Ivory Coast 1-2 L | 2026-03-29 vN Colombia 3-1 W | 2026-03-26 vN Brazil 2-1 W

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Nagelsmann appointed September 2023 and contract extended through 2026.
base_formation: 4-2-3-1 with a double pivot (Goretzka/Pavlovic) and Kimmich at right-back
qualification_path: Won European qualifying group with five wins from six matches
squad_notes: Manuel Neuer reversed his post-Euro 2024 retirement to be named first-choice keeper. Assan Ouedraogo (RB Leipzig) called up to replace the injured Lennart Karl. Bayern Munich supply around seven players. High-profile omissions include Marc-Andre ter Stegen, Serge Gnabry, Niclas Fullkrug, Karim Adeyemi and Robert Andrich. No world-class out-and-out striker in the squad.
warmup_assessment: Encouraging build-up: Germany beat Ghana in a March 2026 friendly and closed preparations with a tune-up against the USA in Chicago; the team looked improved and optimistic relative to recent tournament failures.
shape_verdict: Germany look improved and balanced with a blend of youth and experience, but lack a top-level striker and carry doubts over Musiala's sharpness after his long lay-off.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 @ United States 2-1 W | 2026-05-31 vs Finland 4-0 W | 2026-03-30 vs Ghana 2-1 W | 2026-03-27 @ Switzerland 4-3 W

recent_coach_change: Yes - major recent change. Otto Addo was dismissed in March 2026 and replaced by veteran Portuguese coach Carlos Queiroz, appointed on 14 April 2026, just two months before the World Cup.
base_formation: Built around high intensity, quick transitions and vertical attacks; Queiroz typically favours a compact, organised structure (4-3-3 / 4-2-3-1)
qualification_path: Won CAF Group I with 25 points (8 wins, 1 draw, 1 defeat), finishing ahead of Madagascar and Mali. Sealed qualification on 12 October 2025 with a 1-0 home win over Comoros, Mohammed Kudus scoring the decisive goal.
squad_notes: Queiroz named his final 26-man squad after trimming a 28-man pre-tournament camp in Cardiff, Wales. Star attacker Mohammed Kudus was ruled out injured. Former captain Andre Ayew missed the cut, ending what would have been his fourth World Cup. Centre-back Alexander Djiku also out injured. Antoine Semenyo and Thomas Partey headline the squad.
warmup_assessment: Underwhelming. Ghana drew 1-1 with Wales in Cardiff on 2 June 2026, conceding a stoppage-time equaliser to substitute Lewis Koumas after Caleb Yirenkyi had put them ahead, before flying to Washington to continue preparations.
shape_verdict: Ghana qualified comfortably but arrive destabilised by a March coaching change and the loss of talisman Kudus to injury; a late equaliser conceded against Wales underlined lingering defensive fragility under new boss Queiroz.

recent_coach_change: No recent change immediately before the tournament; Sebastien Migne was appointed in June 2024 and led the qualifying campaign.
qualification_path: Qualified by topping CONCACAF final-round Group C, sealed with a 2-0 win over Nicaragua on the final matchday (played in Curacao). It is Haiti's second-ever World Cup and first since 1974, ending a 52-year drought.
squad_notes: Squad of 26 (3 GK, 8 DF, 7 MF, 8 FW) made up almost entirely of diaspora players from Europe, North America, South America and Asia; only one domestic-league player, midfielder Woodensky Pierre (Violette AC). Young group, average age 24, with only five players over 30. Veteran goalkeeper Johny Placide captains the side.
warmup_assessment: Mixed/limited information; Haiti arranged warm-up friendlies in early June around Fort Lauderdale and Miami (including matches vs New Zealand and Peru) as final preparation. The squad is realistic about its chances per coach Migne.
shape_verdict: Haiti arrive as underdogs and the lowest-profile side in Group C, leaning on a young, diaspora-based squad and the feel-good momentum of ending a 52-year wait, but they look short on tournament pedigree.
warmup_results: 2026-06-05 vN Peru 1-2 L | 2026-06-02 vN New Zealand 4-0 W | 2026-03-31 vN Iceland 1-1 D | 2026-03-28 vN Tunisia 0-1 L

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Ghalenoei has led Team Melli through the entire qualifying campaign in his second stint.
qualification_path: Topped AFC third-round Group A (with Uzbekistan, UAE, Qatar, North Korea, Kyrgyzstan), sealing automatic qualification on March 25, 2025 with a 2-2 home draw vs Uzbekistan; among the first nations to reach the finals. Across full qualifying: P16 W11 D4 L1, 35 goals for, 12 against.
squad_notes: Ghalenoei named a 26-man squad (3 GK, 6 DF, 10 MF, 5 FW) on June 1, 2026, leaning on veterans Taremi and Jahanbakhsh. The headline omission was top scorer Sardar Azmoun. The squad will be based in Tijuana, Mexico after FIFA approved moving the camp from Arizona amid US-Iran political tensions; visa logistics for entering the US for group matches were a live concern.
warmup_assessment: Solid send-off: beat Mali 2-0 in their final pre-tournament friendly, finishing 2026 with three wins and one loss in four matches before departing for their Tijuana base.
shape_verdict: Defensively organized and tournament-tested with a quality leader in Taremi, Iran qualified emphatically and warmed up well, though the Azmoun saga and off-pitch visa/logistics uncertainty cloud their preparation.
warmup_results: 2026-06-04 vN Mali 2-0 W | 2026-05-29 vN Gambia 3-1 W | 2026-03-31 vN Costa Rica 5-0 W | 2026-03-27 vN Nigeria 1-2 L

recent_coach_change: Yes. Graham Arnold took charge in May 2025 with qualification hanging by a thread, replacing Spaniard Jesus Casas.
qualification_path: Qualified via the AFC fourth round and then the inter-confederation play-off, beating Bolivia 2-1 in Monterrey, Mexico on 31 March 2026 to become the 48th and final team to qualify. First World Cup appearance since 1986.
squad_notes: 26-man squad built around the core that survived one of the longest qualifying roads any nation has taken. Mix of veterans (Jalal Hassan 35, Rebin Sulaka 34) and youth (four players aged 22). Heavy reliance on the Iraqi domestic league plus a growing European-based contingent.
warmup_assessment: Encouraging. Iraq held Spain to a 1-1 draw at Estadio de Riazor in La Coruna on 4 June 2026, with Merchas Doski equalising and goalkeeper Ahmed Basil making several key saves; a further warm-up against Venezuela was scheduled.
shape_verdict: Tournament debutants after a 40-year absence and the lowest-ranked team in a tough group, but the spirited draw with Spain suggests they are defensively organised and arrive with momentum and belief under Arnold.

recent_coach_change: No recent change. Fae took over mid-tournament at AFCON 2023 (replacing Jean-Louis Gasset), won the title, and was made permanent in early 2024.
qualification_path: Won CAF qualifying Group F unbeaten, conceding zero goals across 10 matches, edging Gabon by one point
squad_notes: Left-back Clement Akpa (Auxerre) withdrew injured and was replaced by Christopher Operi (Istanbul Basaksehir) on/around 29-30 May. Sebastien Haller (Borussia Dortmund), the AFCON 2023 hero, was a surprise omission, leaving the side without a traditional focal-point striker. Young talents Yan Diomande and Bazoumana Toure included.
warmup_assessment: Strong build-up: wins over Scotland and South Korea, capped by a creditable 1-1 draw away to France in Nantes (4 June 2026), Pepe assisting the equaliser.
shape_verdict: Ivory Coast look settled and confident under Fae with a deep, talented squad and excellent defensive record, though the lack of an established centre-forward after Haller's omission is a question mark.
warmup_results: 2026-06-04 @ France 2-1 W | 2026-03-31 vN Scotland 1-0 W | 2026-03-28 vN South Korea 4-0 W

coach_appointed: 2018 (in charge for a second consecutive World Cup)
recent_coach_change: No recent change; Moriyasu has led Japan since 2018 and through Qatar 2022.
qualification_path: Won AFC Third Round Group C; became the first nation outside the three hosts to qualify, securing their place with a win over Bahrain in March 2025.
key_injuries: Kaoru Mitoma (out); Takumi Minamino (out); Hidemasa Morita (out)
squad_notes: 26-man squad: 3 GK, 9 defenders, 9 midfielders, 5 forwards. Three notable injury absences (Mitoma, Minamino, Morita) which Moriyasu called 'a huge blow'. Veteran Yuto Nagatomo (FC Tokyo) included for a record fifth consecutive World Cup. Squad blends youth with European-based talent across the Premier League, Bundesliga and LaLiga.
warmup_assessment: Positive but unspectacular: edged Iceland 1-0 in their final pre-tournament friendly in Tokyo on June 7, 2026 (Koki Ogawa, 87th minute), in front of 60,000+. Earlier the team posted statement away wins over Scotland and England (1-0 at Wembley in March) before the squad was named.
shape_verdict: A confident, well-organized side that recently beat England away and qualified first of all non-host nations, though the injury losses of Mitoma, Minamino and Morita blunt their attack and a flat win over Iceland showed limited cutting edge.

recent_coach_change: Sellami appointed June 2024 after Hussein Ammouta left for family reasons; Sellami then guided Jordan to its first-ever World Cup qualification.
qualification_path: Qualified directly by finishing second in Group B of the AFC third round with 16 points, behind South Korea; sealed it on 5 June 2025 with a 3-0 away win over Oman (Ali Olwan hat-trick). First-ever World Cup appearance.
key_injuries: Yazan Al-Naimat (out); Ali Olwan (doubt); Adham Al-Qurashi (out)
squad_notes: Historic first World Cup squad, confirmed by Sellami on 2 June. Built on the generation that reached the 2023 Asian Cup final and 2025 Arab Cup final. Key striker Ali Olwan included despite limited match fitness; ACL victim Yazan Al-Naimat misses out.
warmup_assessment: Limited public results before the snapshot date; Jordan are scheduled to play Colombia on 8 June as their main high-profile warm-up. Preparation focused on integrating returning/injured forwards.
shape_verdict: Debutants arrive on a genuine high after first-ever qualification, but injuries to key forwards (Al-Naimat out, Olwan short of fitness) leave them leaning heavily on captain Al-Tamari in a brutal group.
warmup_results: 2026-05-31 @ Switzerland 1-4 L | 2026-03-31 vN Nigeria 2-2 D | 2026-03-27 vN Costa Rica 2-2 D

recent_coach_change: No change in the last 12 months; Aguirre has been in charge through the pre-tournament cycle and named the final 26-man squad on 1 June 2026.
qualification_path: Qualified automatically as co-host of the 2026 World Cup; also won back-to-back CONCACAF titles in the cycle.
squad_notes: Guillermo Ochoa's inclusion for a record sixth World Cup is the headline. 17-year-old Gilberto Mora was called up and could become the youngest Mexican to play at a World Cup. Hirving 'Chucky' Lozano was left out amid disciplinary questions and limited playing time at San Diego FC. Aguirre leaned toward a new generation seeking redemption after the Qatar 2022 group-stage exit.
warmup_assessment: Encouraging pre-tournament form: in March 2026 Mexico drew 0-0 with Portugal at the Estadio Azteca and 1-1 with Belgium (Jorge Sanchez scored), holding two top-10 sides despite missing around 12 regulars to injury. Pundits saw the results as a step in the right direction.
shape_verdict: Mexico head into the home World Cup with positive momentum after creditable draws against Portugal and Belgium and back-to-back CONCACAF titles, blending veterans like Ochoa with a promising new generation, though Santiago Gimenez's fitness after an injury-hit Milan season is a minor question.

recent_coach_change: Yes. Walid Regragui departed in March 2026 (following the AFCON final loss to Senegal and reported friction with the federation). Mohamed Ouahbi, 49, who had led Morocco's U-20 side to the 2025 U-20 World Cup title, was promoted to the senior role just months before the World Cup.
qualification_path: Qualified by winning CAF Group E with a perfect record, becoming the first African nation to secure a 2026 berth, sealing it with two matchdays to spare.
squad_notes: Notable omission of Youssef En-Nesyri (scorer in the 2022 quarter-final). Bright inclusion of 18-year-old Ayyoub Bouaddi (Lille), who switched allegiance from France's youth teams. Squad built around the core of the 2022 semi-final side.
warmup_assessment: Positive. Morocco beat Madagascar 4-0 in Rabat on 2 June (Saibari brace, Rahimi penalty, El Kaabi) after a closed-doors game vs Burundi, and closed preparations against Norway in New Jersey on 7 June; the team looked well-drilled and confident under Ouahbi.
shape_verdict: Morocco look organised and confident going in, riding a perfect qualifying record and strong warm-up form, though the abrupt promotion of an untested senior coach in Ouahbi just before the tournament is a question mark.
warmup_results: 2026-06-02 vs Madagascar 4-0 W | 2026-05-26 vs Burundi 5-0 W | 2026-03-31 vN Paraguay 2-1 W | 2026-03-27 vN Ecuador 1-1 D

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Koeman has been in charge since his 2023 return.
qualification_path: Qualified as winners of UEFA qualifying group; unbeaten through qualifying (six wins, two draws).
key_injuries: Xavi Simons (out); Jeremie Frimpong (out); Memphis Depay (returned)
squad_notes: Koeman delayed the announcement from May 25 to May 27 to assess the fitness of Depay, Jurrien Timber and Justin Kluivert. Shock first-ever senior call-up for West Ham winger Crysencio Summerville, included directly into the World Cup squad. Squad is midfield/forward heavy (eight midfielders, eight forwards) to compensate for the absences of Simons and Jerdy Schouten. Premier League-heavy group (around 15 PL-based players).
warmup_assessment: Mixed-to-poor send-off: lost 1-0 to Algeria in Rotterdam on June 3, 2026, with a late Anis Hadj Moussa winner; Koeman called the defeat a 'wake-up call'. A final friendly vs Uzbekistan was scheduled for June 8 in New York.
shape_verdict: A talented, deep squad and a flawless qualifying record, but the ACL loss of Xavi Simons removes their natural number 10 and the Algeria defeat exposed a lack of sharpness just before kickoff.
warmup_results: 2026-06-03 vs Algeria 0-1 L | 2026-03-31 vs Ecuador 1-1 D | 2026-03-27 vs Norway 2-1 W

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Bazeley has been New Zealand head coach since 2023 and led the OFC qualifying campaign.
qualification_path: Qualified via Oceania (OFC), claiming the region's guaranteed direct slot in the expanded 48-team format; sealed their place with a 3-0 win over New Caledonia at Eden Park in March 2025, after a 7-0 semi-final rout of Fiji (Chris Wood hat-trick).
squad_notes: Bazeley skipped FIFA's extended preliminary squad and named his final 26 directly on May 14, 2026 in Auckland. The squad has 15 players based outside New Zealand/the A-League. Chris Wood and Tommy Smith are the only returnees from 2010, NZ's last World Cup. Bazeley becomes the first person to have coached a FIFA U-17 WC, U-20 WC, Men's Olympic tournament and a senior World Cup.
warmup_assessment: Poor send-off: thrashed 4-0 by Haiti in Fort Lauderdale on June 2, 2026, then lost 1-0 to England in Tampa on June 6 (Harry Kane header). Wood acknowledged the gulf, noting opponents treated the games more seriously than NZ.
shape_verdict: A workmanlike side appearing at its first World Cup in 16 years, captained by Premier League striker Chris Wood, but back-to-back warm-up defeats including a 4-0 loss to Haiti underline how big a step up Group G represents.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN England 0-1 L | 2026-06-02 vN Haiti 0-4 L

recent_coach_change: No recent change. Solbakken has been in charge since December 2020 (succeeding Lars Lagerback); 2026 is widely expected to be his final tournament in charge.
qualification_path: Won UEFA Group I with a perfect record: eight wins from eight, 37 goals for and 5 against, including a 3-0 win over Italy in Oslo and a 4-1 win at the San Siro. First World Cup since 1998, ending a 28-year absence.
squad_notes: 26-man squad unveiled 21 May 2026, fronted by a video message from King Harald V. A genuine golden generation reaching a first major tournament in a generation, built around Haaland and Odegaard with strong attacking depth.
warmup_assessment: Positive. Norway beat Sweden 3-1 on 1 June 2026 (with Haaland rested) and faced Morocco in a further warm-up on 7 June, arriving in good form on the back of a flawless qualifying campaign.
shape_verdict: Riding the momentum of a perfect qualifying run and a tournament-class attack, Norway look in excellent shape as dark horses, with the main caveat being Odegaard's fitness after an injury-hit season.
warmup_results: 2026-06-01 vs Sweden 3-1 W | 2026-03-31 vs Switzerland 0-0 D | 2026-03-27 @ Netherlands 1-2 L

coach_nationality: Denmark/Spain (born in Denmark to a Spanish mother; played internationally for Spain)
recent_coach_change: None recent. Christiansen has led Panama since 2020 and is the country's longest-serving coach in the role.
base_formation: Compact, organised defensive block built on a midfield pivot of Godoy and Carrasquilla
qualification_path: Came through CONCACAF qualifying: won their Second Round group with a perfect 4-0-0 record (12 points, one goal conceded), then topped their Final Round group with a 3-3-0 record to secure a direct berth alongside Curacao and Haiti. Clinched with a 3-0 win over El Salvador on the final matchday (18 November 2025).
key_injuries: Adalberto Carrasquilla (doubt); Anibal Godoy (doubt); Luis Mejia (doubt)
squad_notes: Christiansen named his 26-man squad on 26 May 2026 at the Panama Canal Administration Building. The selection blends seven 2018 World Cup veterans (Murillo, Godoy, Escobar, Barcenas, Mosquera among them) with a younger generation. Captain Anibal Godoy is the record cap-holder; Carrasquilla is the creative focal point.
warmup_assessment: Mixed and tough. Panama were heavily beaten 6-2 by Brazil in Rio (31 May), then bounced back with a 4-2 win over the Dominican Republic, before a final warm-up against Bosnia and Herzegovina in St. Louis on 6 June. Earlier in March they drew 1-1 and beat South Africa 2-1.
shape_verdict: Panama qualified solidly and have a settled, experienced core, but a chastening 6-2 loss to Brazil and fitness doubts over key men Carrasquilla and Godoy temper expectations going into a daunting group.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Bosnia and Herzegovina 1-1 D | 2026-06-03 vs Dominican Republic 4-2 W | 2026-05-31 @ Brazil 2-6 L | 2026-03-31 @ South Africa 2-1 W | 2026-03-27 @ South Africa 1-1 D | 2026-01-22 vs Mexico 0-1 L | 2026-01-18 @ Bolivia 1-1 D

recent_coach_change: Alfaro took charge in August 2024 and rebuilt the team; no change since.
base_formation: Floating 3-4-3 (three attacking midfielders behind a centre-forward)
qualification_path: Qualified via CONMEBOL, claiming the last automatic South American spot; finished fifth in the group, 10 points behind winners Argentina. Sealed qualification on September 4, 2025, with a goalless draw against Ecuador in Asuncion.
squad_notes: Alfaro named the 26-man squad on June 1, 2026, marking La Albirroja's return after a 16-year absence (longest ever). Manchester United's Diego Leon, despite being capped, was not included in the final selection. Squad blends Europe- and South America-based players with veteran spine around captain Gustavo Gomez.
warmup_assessment: Positive send-off. Paraguay beat Nicaragua 4-0 at their CARDE high-performance centre on June 5, 2026. Earlier 2026 friendlies were mixed (1-0 win over Greece on March 27, 1-2 loss to Morocco on March 31).
shape_verdict: A gritty, physically robust side built on defensive solidity (10 clean sheets in qualifying) but a modest goal threat. Returning to the World Cup with momentum and confidence, though reliant on Enciso's fitness for creativity.
warmup_results: 2026-06-05 vs Nicaragua 4-0 W | 2026-03-31 vN Morocco 1-2 L | 2026-03-27 @ Greece 1-0 W

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Martinez has been in charge since January 2023 and led qualification.
qualification_path: Qualified through UEFA qualifying as group winners; also won the 2024-25 UEFA Nations League. Reached a record sixth World Cup for Cristiano Ronaldo.
squad_notes: Martinez named a 26-man squad plus a symbolic 'plus one' place in memory of Diogo Jota, who was killed in a car crash in July 2025. Reserve goalkeeper Ricardo Velho travels as cover. Stacked attack and a PSG midfield core (Vitinha, Joao Neves, Nuno Mendes).
warmup_assessment: Beat Chile 2-1 on 6 June 2026 (Guedes and Bruno Fernandes scored) but Rafael Leao was sent off and Ronaldo was withdrawn at half-time. A second friendly against Nigeria is set for 10 June.
shape_verdict: Portugal arrive among the tournament favourites with elite depth and Nations League momentum, though the Leao red card and Ronaldo's age are minor clouds over an otherwise strong, deep squad.

recent_coach_change: Yes — Lopetegui was appointed in May 2025, replacing Marquez Lopez, and led Qatar through the final qualifying rounds.
qualification_path: Qualified on merit for the first time via standard AFC qualifying: won their second-round group (16 points from 6 games), advanced through the third round, then came through the fourth round (drew Oman, beat UAE 2-1).
squad_notes: Lopetegui announced the final 26 on June 2, 2026, spearheaded by Afif and Almoez Ali. The squad was trimmed from a 35-man preliminary list (May 18) down through 28 (May 25). Goalkeeper Shehab Al Laithi and midfielder Rayan Al Ali were among the late cuts. The squad is overwhelmingly domestic, developed through the Aspire Academy system.
warmup_assessment: Modest: Qatar lost 1-0 to the Republic of Ireland in a warm-up friendly in late May 2026 before final cuts, then continued high-intensity training on the US west coast (Santa Barbara/California).
shape_verdict: A cohesive, Aspire-built squad enjoying a historic first merit-based qualification, but depth and quality against elite opposition are unproven and warm-up form (a 1-0 loss to Ireland) was tepid.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN El Salvador 0-0 D | 2026-05-28 @ Republic of Ireland 0-1 L

coach_appointed: 2026-04 (appointed weeks before the finals after Renard's dismissal)
recent_coach_change: Yes — the Saudi Football Federation sacked Herve Renard on 17 April 2026 and appointed Greek coach Georgios Donis just two months before the tournament.
qualification_path: Reached the finals via the AFC fourth round, finishing first in their group ahead of Iraq and Indonesia after failing to qualify directly in the earlier third round (finished third behind Japan and Australia).
squad_notes: Major late managerial upheaval with Donis replacing Renard. Final 26-man squad confirmed ahead of FIFA's 2 June deadline (preliminary 30 on 23 May, final on 31 May). Squad is almost entirely domestic-based (only Saud Abdulhamid plays abroad). Veteran Salman Al-Faraj left out by the new coach.
warmup_assessment: Encouraging under the new boss: Saudi Arabia recorded a 3-0 win over Puerto Rico in Austin (their first under Donis), a match disrupted for nearly two hours by severe weather, with goals from Mandash, Al-Hamddan and Al-Dawsari.
shape_verdict: A domestically based side rebuilding under a coach appointed only weeks ago; a tidy 3-0 warm-up win offers some optimism but the late coaching change and a scrappy qualification leave them as clear group underdogs against Spain and Uruguay.
warmup_results: 2026-06-05 vN Puerto Rico 3-0 W | 2026-05-30 vN Ecuador 1-2 L | 2026-03-31 @ Serbia 1-2 L | 2026-03-27 vs Egypt 0-4 L

recent_coach_change: No. Steve Clarke has been in charge since May 2019 and led Scotland to a first World Cup since 1998.
qualification_path: Qualified as winners of UEFA qualifying Group C, sealed with a 4-2 home win over Denmark at Hampden Park in a winner-takes-all final group game, ending a 28-year World Cup absence.
squad_notes: Steve Clarke named a 26-man squad. Notable inclusion of Ross Stewart (Southampton), ending four years out of the international set-up, plus 19-year-old Findlay Curtis (Kilmarnock). Veteran goalkeeper Craig Gordon (Hearts) selected. Some tough calls were made on Hibs and Hearts players.
warmup_assessment: Excellent. Scotland beat Curacao 4-1 at Hampden on 30 May (Shankland brace) and thrashed Bolivia 4-0 in New Jersey on 6 June (Che Adams brace, McTominay, Shankland), a perfect confidence boost in their final tune-up.
shape_verdict: Scotland look in fine form and high spirits, with two emphatic warm-up wins and a settled, experienced spine, going into their first World Cup in 28 years on a clear high.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Bolivia 4-0 W | 2026-05-30 vs Curaçao 4-1 W | 2026-03-31 vN Ivory Coast 0-1 L | 2026-03-28 vs Japan 0-1 L

recent_coach_change: Yes. Pape Thiaw was appointed on 13 December 2024, replacing Aliou Cisse who had been dismissed about two months earlier.
qualification_path: Won CAF Group B unbeaten with 24 points from 30 (seven wins, three draws), finishing two points ahead of DR Congo; sealed top spot with a 4-0 win over Mauritania.
squad_notes: Final 26 trimmed from a 28-man preliminary list; Ilay Camara and Moustapha Mbow were the two players cut after the USA friendly. Malang Sarr was omitted, with Thiaw explaining the decision publicly. Core of the 2022 AFCON-winning golden generation, several of whom are likely at their final World Cup.
warmup_assessment: Underwhelming. Senegal lost 3-2 to the United States on 31 May 2026, with a wobbly defensive display, and faced Saudi Arabia on 9 June. Preparations were further disrupted by an off-pitch contract standoff involving coach Pape Thiaw.
shape_verdict: A talented, experienced squad with one of the deepest player pools at the tournament, but defensive frailty in the USA friendly and coaching-contract turmoil cloud their build-up.
warmup_results: 2026-05-31 @ United States 2-3 L | 2026-03-31 vs Gambia 3-1 W | 2026-03-28 vN Peru 2-0 W

recent_coach_change: No change; Hugo Broos has led Bafana Bafana since May 2021 and the 2026 World Cup is set to be his final role as a manager.
qualification_path: Won CAF Group C ahead of Nigeria, sealing first place with a 3-0 win over Rwanda in Mbombela on 14 October 2025; first World Cup appearance in 16 years (since 2010 as host).
squad_notes: Broos named a 26-man squad on 27 May 2026 in Pretoria alongside President Cyril Ramaphosa, picking a largely tried-and-tested group. Nineteen of the 26 are domestically based, with Mamelodi Sundowns and Orlando Pirates supplying eight players each. The team's departure for Mexico was delayed by a visa issue.
warmup_assessment: Poor warm-up form: South Africa drew 1-1 with Jamaica (Lyle Foster scoring) in their final warm-up, played behind closed doors after a delayed arrival, and earlier Foster missed a penalty in a frustrating result against Nicaragua. Bafana entered the tournament winless in their last five matches.
shape_verdict: South Africa reach their first World Cup in 16 years on shaky form, winless in five warm-up matches and disrupted by a visa-related travel delay, though a disciplined, mostly home-based squad under departing coach Hugo Broos still carries belief.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Jamaica 1-0 W | 2026-05-29 vs Nicaragua 0-0 D | 2026-03-31 vs Panama 1-2 L | 2026-03-27 vs Panama 1-1 D

recent_coach_change: No coaching change in the last 12 months; Hong Myung-bo led the team through AFC qualifying and named the World Cup squad.
qualification_path: Qualified directly via AFC third round, topping Group C with five wins and one draw (16 points, unbeaten) ahead of nations including China, Thailand and Singapore; an 11th consecutive World Cup appearance.
squad_notes: Final squad revealed on 16 May 2026. Son Heung-min named captain for his fourth World Cup. Cho Wi-je was called up on 2 June 2026 as an injury replacement for Cho Yu-min. No injury concerns reported for Son himself.
warmup_assessment: Mixed but ultimately positive: South Korea earned a narrow 1-0 win over El Salvador in their final World Cup warm-up on 4 June 2026 in Provo, Utah, decided by a Lee Dong-gyeong free-kick. Hong acknowledged early offensive difficulties but called the match useful preparation.
shape_verdict: South Korea arrive in solid shape after dominant, unbeaten AFC qualifying and a winning final warm-up, anchored by captain Son Heung-min and a core of European-based players, though they were not fully fluent offensively in the El Salvador friendly.
warmup_results: 2026-06-03 vN El Salvador 1-0 W | 2026-05-30 vN Trinidad and Tobago 5-0 W | 2026-03-31 @ Austria 0-1 L | 2026-03-28 vN Ivory Coast 0-4 L

coach_appointed: 2022-12 (took over after Luis Enrique following the 2022 World Cup)
recent_coach_change: No recent change; De la Fuente has been in charge since late 2022 and won Euro 2024.
qualification_path: Qualified as winners of UEFA qualifying Group E, taking 16 of a possible 18 points (5 wins, 1 draw).
key_injuries: Lamine Yamal (doubt); Nico Williams (doubt); Victor Munoz (doubt)
squad_notes: No Real Madrid players were included in the final 26. Rodri named captain; goalkeepers are Unai Simon (Athletic Club), David Raya (Arsenal) and Joan Garcia (Barcelona), with Alex Remiro left out. Gavi returns to the squad after recovering from a serious knee injury.
warmup_assessment: Underwhelming on the scoreline: a heavily rotated Spain were held to a 1-1 draw by Iraq in La Coruna on June 5, 2026 (Ferran Torres scored), with De la Fuente resting most first-choice players and handing several youngsters debuts.
shape_verdict: Spain arrive as one of the tournament favourites with an elite, deep squad, though several key attackers are managing knocks and a tired post-Champions-League core means fitness management is the main concern.

recent_coach_change: Yes — Jon Dahl Tomasson was sacked on October 14, 2025 after a 1-0 home loss to Kosovo; Graham Potter was appointed six days later, on October 20, 2025.
qualification_path: Reached the finals via the playoffs. Sweden finished last in their qualifying group (two points, behind Switzerland, Slovenia and Kosovo) but earned a playoff lifeline through their UEFA Nations League group win, then beat Ukraine 3-1 (Gyokeres hat-trick) in the semifinal and Poland 3-2 in the final.
squad_notes: 26-man squad announced mid-May 2026 headlined by strike pairing Alexander Isak and Viktor Gyokeres. Defender Emil Holm withdrew injured and was replaced on May 30 by FC Dallas defender Herman Johansson, who had narrowly missed the original cut. Note: sources variously list Holm at Juventus/Bologna.
warmup_assessment: Underwhelming send-off: drew 2-2 at home to Greece on June 4, 2026 at Friends Arena, conceding a stoppage-time equalizer to Giorgos Masouras after Gyokeres and Gustaf Nilsson goals had put them ahead. Isak and Gyokeres both started.
shape_verdict: Loaded with elite attacking talent in Isak and Gyokeres but arriving on the back of a chaotic playoff qualification and a new coach in Potter, with defensive questions underlined by the Holm withdrawal and a late-conceded draw with Greece.

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Yakin retained after a strong Euro 2024 run and leads Switzerland into a sixth consecutive World Cup.
qualification_path: Qualified directly as winners of UEFA Group B, finishing top with 14 points (ahead of Kosovo on 11), unbeaten and three points clear.
key_injuries: Breel Embolo (doubt); Zeki Amdouni (returned); Fabian Rieder (doubt); Noah Okafor (doubt)
squad_notes: Switzerland confirmed their 26 on May 20, 2026 (rolled out via social media May 18-19). Yakin trusted an experienced core led by Xhaka and Akanji. The most discussed call was including Zeki Amdouni, who barely played for Burnley after a serious ACL injury.
warmup_assessment: Mixed: Switzerland dominated possession but drew 1-1 with Australia in San Diego on June 6, 2026 (Dan Ndoye scoring off a Xhaka assist before Tete Yengi equalized following a defensive error), failing to convert clear superiority.
shape_verdict: An experienced, settled side that qualified comfortably and looks structurally sound in a 4-2-3-1, though the Australia draw and Embolo's travel uncertainty leave minor question marks on the attack.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Australia 1-1 D | 2026-05-31 vs Jordan 4-1 W | 2026-03-31 @ Norway 0-0 D | 2026-03-27 vs Germany 3-4 L

coach_appointed: Early 2026 (after the Africa Cup of Nations)
recent_coach_change: Yes — Lamouchi succeeded Sami Trabelsi following a disappointing AFCON showing earlier in 2026. Lamouchi credited Trabelsi and the players for the qualifying campaign run under the previous regime.
qualification_path: Won CAF qualifying Group H, taking 9 wins from 10 matches without conceding a single goal across the entire campaign.
squad_notes: Lamouchi named a much-changed 26-man squad (announced mid-May 2026) compared to recent campaigns. Defensive identity is the team's calling card, built on Montassar Talbi, Dylan Bronn and goalkeeper Aymen Dahmene. This is Tunisia's third consecutive World Cup appearance; they have never advanced past the group stage in six previous campaigns (beat France at Qatar 2022 but went out on goal difference).
warmup_assessment: Poor: hammered 5-0 by Belgium in their final World Cup warm-up, a heavy defeat that exposed the gap between their clean-sheet qualifying form and elite opposition. Lamouchi warned his defense to expect far more intense competition at the World Cup.
shape_verdict: Defensively excellent during qualifying (record clean-sheet run) but the chastening 5-0 friendly loss to Belgium and a relatively new coach raise doubts about whether that record holds against top-tier attacks.

recent_coach_change: Montella appointed September 21, 2023, replacing the sacked Stefan Kuntz; led Turkey to Euro 2024 quarterfinals and 2026 World Cup qualification. No change in the last 12 months.
base_formation: Balanced setup blending Europe-based stars with domestic talent (attacking, possession-oriented)
qualification_path: Qualified via UEFA play-offs after the European group stage: beat Romania 1-0 in the semifinal in Istanbul (Ferdi Kadioglu goal), then won the final 1-0 away at Kosovo in Pristina on March 31, 2026 (Kerem Akturkoglu). First World Cup appearance since 2002.
squad_notes: Montella named the final 26-man squad on June 2, 2026, sealing Turkey's return after a 24-year wait. Goalkeepers are Altay Bayindir (Manchester United), Mert Gunok (Fenerbahce) and Ugurcan Cakir (Galatasaray). Three standby players (GK Muhammed Sengezer, Aral Simsir, Demir Ege Tiknaz) were excluded from the 26 but travel as injury replacements. Optimism centers on the young generation led by Guler and Yildiz.
warmup_assessment: Encouraging warm-ups. Turkey beat Venezuela 2-1 at Inter Miami's stadium on June 7, 2026 (Akgun goal plus an own goal), with Arda Guler featuring, and earlier defeated North Macedonia 4-0, boosting confidence before the opener.
shape_verdict: An exciting, attack-minded side built around a golden generation (Guler, Yildiz, Calhanoglu) arriving with momentum after positive friendlies, though both key playmakers carried late-season muscle injuries and fitness/sharpness is a mild concern.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vN Venezuela 2-1 W | 2026-06-01 vs North Macedonia 4-0 W

recent_coach_change: Pochettino appointed September 2024, replacing interim Mikey Varas after Gregg Berhalter's departure following Copa America 2024; no change in the last 12 months.
base_formation: Back three (3-4-2-1 / 3-4-3 variants tested through qualifying and friendlies)
qualification_path: Automatic qualification as co-host (USA, Canada, Mexico host the 2026 World Cup).
key_injuries: Johnny Cardoso (out); Chris Richards (doubt); Patrick Agyemang (out)
squad_notes: Pochettino named a 26-man roster on May 26, 2026, in New York. Gio Reyna and Joe Scally (both Borussia Monchengladbach) and Club America's Alejandro Zendejas were notable inclusions; Real Salt Lake's Diego Luna was a surprise omission, along with Tanner Tessmann and Aidan Morris, leaving Tyler Adams as the lone specialist defensive midfielder. 13 players are first-time World Cup participants; 13 returnees from 2022. All three goalkeepers are MLS-based.
warmup_assessment: Mixed final tune-ups. The USA lost 2-1 to Germany at Soldier Field on June 6, 2026 (Mark McKenzie deputizing for the injured Richards), with earlier 2026 friendlies producing varied results; performances suggested unsettled defensive personnel due to injuries.
shape_verdict: A talented host nation with its strongest attacking core (Pulisic, McKennie, Balogun) but unsettled at defensive midfield after losing Cardoso and with Richards a fitness doubt at center-back. Tactically still flexible under Pochettino but not yet convincingly cohesive.
warmup_results: 2026-06-06 vs Germany 1-2 L | 2026-05-31 vs Senegal 3-2 W | 2026-03-31 vs Portugal 0-2 L | 2026-03-28 vs Belgium 2-5 L

recent_coach_change: No recent change; Bielsa has led Uruguay since May 2023, though his position is under pressure amid reported dressing-room discontent.
qualification_path: Qualified directly from CONMEBOL, finishing fourth with 28 points (7 wins, 7 draws, 4 defeats from 18 matches).
squad_notes: Luis Suarez is the most notable omission, missing a World Cup for the first time since 2010. Veteran goalkeeper Fernando Muslera returns from international retirement for a fifth World Cup, set to become Uruguay's most-capped World Cup player. Final 26-man squad announced May 31, 2026.
warmup_assessment: Worrying. Uruguay did not play an early-June friendly; their most recent results were a 1-1 draw with England and a 0-0 draw with Algeria in March 2026, extending a winless run, following a chastening 5-1 friendly loss to the USA in November 2025.
shape_verdict: Talented and experienced on paper, but Uruguay enter with poor recent form and a backdrop of reported player discontent with Bielsa's demanding methods, raising doubts about morale and cohesion.

recent_coach_change: Yes. Timur Kapadze guided Uzbekistan to qualification, then Italian World Cup winner Fabio Cannavaro was appointed in October 2025, inheriting an already-qualified side; Kapadze stepped back into the staff.
qualification_path: Finished second in their AFC third-round qualifying group behind Iran (ahead of UAE, Qatar, Kyrgyzstan and North Korea), sealing qualification with a goalless draw away to the UAE in June 2025. First-ever World Cup and first Central Asian nation to qualify.
squad_notes: Cannavaro announced the 26-man squad on 2 June 2026, hours before FIFA's deadline. Squad is defence-heavy (3 GKs, 13 defenders, 6 midfielders, 7 forwards) and largely domestic-based, with Khusanov at Manchester City the standout name playing in elite European football.
warmup_assessment: Lost 2-0 to Canada in Edmonton in their first warm-up (Osorio and Nelson scored, Khusanov played the full match); a final friendly against the Netherlands was set for 8 June 2026.
shape_verdict: Tournament debutants arriving with modest expectations: well-organised defensively and buoyed by their historic qualification, but thin on elite attacking quality and tested by stronger opponents in warm-ups.
